_comment: Source: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 (March 2022). Cardiovascular: Suspected Stroke/Transient Ischemic Attack. NASEMSO national provisional base.
level_display: All Levels — Interventions Scoped Per Provider Level
protocol_reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Cardiovascular: Suspected Stroke/Transient Ischemic Attack

sections.title: Patient Care Goals
sections.reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Cardiovascular: Suspected Stroke/Transient Ischemic Attack
points: Detect neurological deficits
Determine eligibility for transport to a stroke center
Identify patients who have potentially sustained a stroke involving a large vessel occlusion (LVO)

sections.title: Patient Presentation
points: Patient has signs and symptoms consistent with stroke or transient ischemic attack (TIA)
EXCLUSION CRITERIA:
If glucose less than 60 mg/dL (deciliter), treat per the Hypoglycemia Guideline
If trauma and Glasgow Coma Score (GCS) less than or equal to 13, treat per the Head Injury Guideline and General Trauma Management Guideline

sections.title: EMR Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMR level (Emergency Medical Responder)
points: Cincinnati or FAST stroke screen: Face droop, Arm drift, Speech abnormality, Time
Supine position with head of bed elevated 15-30 degrees (avoid flat)
Oxygen if SpO2 <94%; avoid supplemental O2 if SpO2 >94%
CPR/AED if patient becomes pulseless
Protect paralyzed extremities; do not restrain

sections.title: EMT Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMT level (Emergency Medical Technician)
points: CPSS or LAMS stroke scale assessment; document last known well time
12-lead ECG acquisition (rule out cardiac cause of symptoms)
Blood glucose check — treat hypoglycemia if present
IV access maintenance (non-medicated)
Oral glucose only if hypoglycemia confirmed and patient can swallow safely

sections.title: AEMT Interventions
points: Blood glucose check; IV D50W 25 g or D10W for documented hypoglycemia
IV access initiation; IV fluid at KVO rate (do not over-hydrate)
Waveform capnography for airway monitoring
IO access if IV unsuccessful and airway compromise

sections.title: Paramedic Interventions
points: 12-lead ECG interpretation to rule out dysrhythmia as stroke mimic
Advance notification to stroke center: last known well time, stroke scale score, estimated arrival
Blood glucose — if <60 mg/dL: D50W 25 g IV
Airway management: intubate only if airway compromise (avoid unnecessary intubation)
Avoid dextrose-containing IV fluids (except for documented hypoglycemia)
Control seizures: benzodiazepine per seizure protocol
BP management: do not aggressively lower BP in acute ischemic stroke unless >220/120 or tPA candidate >185/110
Los Angeles Motor Scale (LAMS) ≥4 suggests large vessel occlusion: direct to comprehensive stroke center when available

sections.title: Patient Safety Considerations
points: Prevent aspiration ' elevate head of stretcher 15'30 degrees if systolic BP greater than 100 mmHg a. Maintain head and neck in neutral alignment, without flexing the neck
Protect paralyzed limbs from injury
Avoid multiple IV attempts

sections.title: Educational Pearls
points: Transport and destination decisions should be based on local resources and stroke system of care a. Destination hospitals may include: i. Stroke Ready

Time of onset of stroke or last known well is critical data for patient treatment a. Positive stroke scale with time of onset or last known well less than 4. hours may be eligible for thrombolytic agents
Do not treat hypertension
Place on cardiac monitor
Pediatrics: a. Treatment principles remain the same

sections.title: Key Documentation
points: 'Last known well' must be specific o If the patient was last known well prior to bedtime the night before, this is the time to be documented (not time the patient woke up with symptoms present)
Blood glucose results
Specific validated stroke scale used and findings
Time of notification to receiving hospital

EMR: Scene safety, primary survey, CPR, AED, BVM, bleeding control, oral airway, O2 therapy, epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis, naloxone unit-dose auto-injector
EMT: EMR scope PLUS: NPA, supraglottic airway, CPAP, pulse oximetry, 12-lead ECG acquisition, oral glucose, aspirin, patient own nitroglycerin, albuterol SVN, IM epinephrine, blood glucose monitoring, cervical collar, traction splint
AEMT: EMT scope PLUS: waveform capnography, IO access, IV access initiation, IV/IO fluids, IV medications (dextrose, naloxone, epinephrine, glucagon, analgesics, antiemetics), subcutaneous/intranasal medications, nitroglycerin from stock
Paramedic: AEMT scope PLUS: ET intubation, RSI, cricothyrotomy, needle decompression, manual defibrillation, cardioversion, transcutaneous pacing, 12-lead ECG interpretation, all antidysrhythmics, vasopressors, all medication routes and classes

deterioration_flags: Contact medical direction for clinical deterioration or interventions outside standard scope
scope_notes: EMR scope is the minimum national standard — state/MCA may expand
All interventions subject to local medical director credentialing
Pediatric patients: use weight-based dosing and age-appropriate equipment
Contact medical direction when required by local protocol or for interventions outside standard scope
